results.possibleIdentity: Jackson Mac Low (1922–2004) was an American avant-garde artist, poet, and composer who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his experimental poetry and compositions created using chance operations, often in collaboration with other artists and engineers. Mac Low was significantly involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that promoted collaborations between the arts and sciences. He participated in E.A.T.'s landmark 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum and collaborated with engineers from Information International, Inc. to create computer-generated poems. His work is characterized by the use of algorithms and aleatoric (chance-based) methods, which he termed 'simultaneities.' His daughter, Clarinda Mac Low, is also an artist and organizer who has served as a director for E.A.T. and continues to promote the organization's legacy.

results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg (died 2018) was a mathematical and musical theorist who collaborated with synthesizer pioneer Robert Moog in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for designing an experimental 31-tone keyboard and a "Classroom Synthesizer" (c. 1970), which were intended to explore human pattern perception and harmonic scales. His research into pattern recognition and perception during this period was funded in part by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research and is considered a precursor to modern developments in artificial intelligence. His work and correspondence from the 1960s and 1970s are preserved in the Robert Moog Papers at Cornell University, reflecting the interdisciplinary spirit of collaboration between artists, scientists, and engineers that characterized organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

David Rothenberg (born 1933) is a prominent New York theater producer, press agent, and activist who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. In 1966, he produced the anti-war musical "Viet Rock" and in 1967 he produced the influential prison drama "Fortune and Men’s Eyes." The latter project led him to found The Fortune Society in 1967, a non-profit organization dedicated to prisoner rights and successful reentry. As a publicist, he worked with major figures like Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton. While primarily focused on theater and social justice, his founding of a service-oriented non-profit during the late 1960s in the New York cultural scene aligns with the organizational landscape of the era, which included groups like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Allen is an artist, illustrator, photographer, and food historian who was a prominent figure in the "Golden Age of New Paltz" art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. As a student at SUNY New Paltz (graduating around 1967) and later as an independent artist, he was an associate of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that fostered collaborations between the arts and engineering sectors. His work during this era often integrated scientific and mathematical principles, such as using mathematics to generate color sequences for paintings and designing custom lighting systems. Allen notably collaborated with printmaking professor Robert Schuler on a business venture creating experimental buildings made of sprayed urethane foam. In 1968, he was part of the 'midwife' crew that assisted artist Manuel Bromberg in creating the iconic 'Cliffside' sculpture at SUNY New Paltz, a tribute to Martin Luther King Jr. that utilized innovative industrial molding techniques. During the 1970s and 1980s, Allen was also active in the mail art and copy art movements, producing works utilizing Xerox, rubber stamps, and airbrushing. In his later career, he transitioned into food history and writing, authoring or editing approximately 40 books, including 'The Herbalist in the Kitchen' and 'The Oxford Encyclopedia of Food and Drink in America.'

results.possibleIdentity: Hardy Jones (1943–2018) was a prominent filmmaker, journalist, and conservationist whose work in the late 1960s and 1970s often bridged the gaps between media, technology, and science. A graduate of Tulane University and a former journalist for CBS News and United Press International, Jones was a self-described student of Buckminster Fuller, a key figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. In the late 1960s, he worked at CBS, notably appearing in the credits for the 1969 moon landing broadcast alongside Buckminster Fuller. During the 1970s, Jones pivoted toward interdisciplinary filmmaking, collaborating with Michael Wiese and sound engineer Stephan Gagne on projects like the 1978 film 'Dolphin,' which utilized experimental technology such as underwater keyboards to facilitate human-cetacean interaction. His work was characterized by the use of cutting-edge tools to explore consciousness and environmental themes, aligning with the interdisciplinary mission of E.A.T. In 2000, he co-founded the organization BlueVoice.org with actor Ted Danson.

Dr. Hardy Jones (c. 1946–2018) was an orthopedic surgeon and 'found art' sculptor based in Los Altos, California. Though primarily a medical professional who served as Chief of Staff at Santa Clara Kaiser, Jones maintained a passion for creating 'orthopedic art' from scrap metal and antique farm equipment. While he began his public art career in 1983 with the sculpture 'Circles in Motion,' he had been collecting and manipulating metal since his youth. His work, which he termed 'the ultimate in recycling,' consists of over 300 life-sized metal sculptures of animals and mascots found throughout the San Francisco Peninsula. While his work involves the intersection of industrial materials (scrap metal) and artistic form, there is no direct verified link between him and the New York-based Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization from the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Smith (born 1951) is an American video, performance, and installation artist widely recognized for his eponymous performance persona, "Mike," an "Everyman" character who often navigates a world of failed dreams and consumerist trends. Smith's career spans several decades, with his early artistic activity dating back to the late 1960s and early 1970s. He enrolled at Colorado College in 1968 and was a participant in the Whitney Museum of American Art Independent Study Program in 1970 and 1973. His work frequently explores the intersections of art, television, and technology, often employing a deadpan or satirical approach. He is associated with the historical context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through various documentary and archival projects; for instance, his work has been featured by ARTPIX, an organization that collaborates with E.A.T. to archive and present early media art. Additionally, Smith has collaborated extensively with Joshua White, a pioneer of the Joshua Light Show at the Fillmore East in the late 1960s, whose technological experiments were contemporary with and similar in spirit to E.A.T. projects. Smith has exhibited at major institutions including the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum, and the New Museum, and was included in the Pictures Generation exhibition at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

Michael Townsend Smith (born 1935) is an influential American theater critic, playwright, and director who was a central figure in the New York avant-garde and Off-Off-Broadway scenes during the late 1960s and early 1970s. As the chief theater critic for The Village Voice from 1959 to 1974, he was one of the first and most prominent writers to champion experimental theater and performance art, documenting seminal events such as those organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the 9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering performances in 1966. Beyond his criticism, he was an active artist who wrote and directed numerous plays at avant-garde venues like the Caffe Cino, Judson Poets' Theatre, and La MaMa, and he briefly managed the Caffe Cino following the death of its founder. His writing and directing work from this period reflected the radical, often technology-infused experimentation of the era's counterculture. In the late 1970s, he moved to California, where he continued his career as a writer, editor, and teacher.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American artist known for her work in conceptual art, land art, and environmental art. She emerged as a primary figure in the late 1960s and 1970s, with her practice often incorporating science, philosophy, linguistics, and technology. Denes was involved with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from 1969 show her submitting proposals and membership forms. Her early work focused on 'Visual Philosophy' and used technical tools such as X-rays and microscopes. In 1970, she participated in the influential 'Software' exhibition at the Jewish Museum, where she collaborated with the computer scientist group R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. on computational experiments and presented her work 'Matrix of Knowledge'. Other major projects from this period include 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (begun in 1968), which is considered one of the first site-specific ecological artworks. She also collaborated with Bell Laboratories and has been a research fellow at M.I.T. and Carnegie Mellon University.
